elements.sent-2411-001: While large language models (LLMs) have achieved notable success in generative tasks, they still face limitations, such as lacking up-to-date knowledge and producing hallucinations.
elements.sent-2411-002: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) enhances LLM performance by integrating external knowledge bases, providing additional context which significantly improves accuracy and knowledge coverage.
elements.sent-2411-003: However, building these external knowledge bases often requires substantial resources and may involve sensitive information.
elements.sent-2411-004: In this paper, we propose an agent-based automated privacy attack called RAG-Thief, which can extract a scalable amount of private data from the private database used in RAG applications.
elements.sent-2411-005: We conduct a systematic study on the privacy risks associated with RAG applications, revealing that the vulnerability of LLMs makes the private knowledge bases suffer significant privacy risks.
elements.sent-2411-006: Unlike previous manual attacks which rely on traditional prompt injection techniques, RAG-Thief starts with an initial adversarial query and learns from model responses, progressively generating new queries to extract as many chunks from the knowledge base as possible.
elements.sent-2411-007: Experimental results show that our RAG-Thief can extract over 70\% information from the private knowledge bases within customized RAG applications deployed on local machines and real-world platforms, including OpenAI's GPTs and ByteDance's Coze.
elements.sent-2411-008: Our findings highlight the privacy vulnerabilities in current RAG applications and underscore the pressing need for stronger safeguards.

elements.sent-2411-009: Despite the impressive performance of large language models (LLMs) in tasks like knowledge-based question answering and content generation, they still face limitations in specific areas, such as generating hallucinations \cite{ji2023survey , \cite{shuster2021retrieval and lacking access to the most current data.}}
elements.sent-2411-010: The emergence of Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) \cite{lewis2020retrieval , \cite{shi2024replug , \cite{ram2023context , \cite{van2024adapted , \cite{karpukhin2020dense , \cite{borgeaud2022improving , \cite{thoppilan2022lamda expands the capabilities of LLMs and becomes a popular method to enhanc their performance.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-011: RAG integrates information retrieval with text generation by using a retrieval module to extract the most relevant information chunks from external knowledge bases.
elements.sent-2411-012: These chunks are then used as contextual prompts for the language model, improving its ability to produce more accurate, relevant, and coherent responses.
elements.sent-2411-013: Currently, RAG technology is widely applied across various vertical industries, demonstrating significant value in fields like healthcare (e.g., SMART Health GPT\cite{al2023transforming,wang2024potential ), finance \cite{loukas2023making , law (AutoLaw \cite{mahari2021autolaw,kuppa2023chain ) , and scientific research (MyCrunchGPT\cite{kumar2023mycrunchgpt , \cite{boyko2023interdisciplinary , \cite{prince2024opportunities ) .}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-014: For instance, in healthcare, RAG can be combined with proprietary case knowledge bases to build intelligent question-answering systems.
elements.sent-2411-015: These systems not only provide more precise medical analyses but also offer personalized healthcare guidance.
elements.sent-2411-016: By supplementing knowledge with the latest medical literature and case data, such systems can assist doctors and patients in making more informed decisions.
elements.sent-2411-017: Moreover, OpenAI allows users to build and publish GPTs, a type of AI application, with private data.
elements.sent-2411-018: Currently, there are over 3 million custom GPTs on the ChatGPT platform.
elements.sent-2411-019: Intuitively, RAG systems should be relatively secure in terms of privacy, as the private knowledge base is merely an independent external file within the RAG system, and users can only interact with the LLM without direct access to the knowledge base content.
elements.sent-2411-020: However, some studies indicate that RAG systems pose data privacy risks related to the leakage of private knowledge bases.
elements.sent-2411-021: In practice, through prompt injection attacks and multi-turn interactions with LLM, attackers can gradually extract information snippets from the knowledge base by crafting carefully designed questions.
elements.sent-2411-022: Qi et al. \cite{qi2024follow propose a prompt injection attack template using anchor question queries to retrieve the most relevant chunks from the private knowledge base.}
elements.sent-2411-023: However, this method has a low success rate in the absence of relevant domain knowledge, achieving only a 3.22\% success rate in simulated environments.
elements.sent-2411-024: Another recent study by Zeng et al. \cite{zeng-etal-2024-good introduces a structured query format designed to target and extract specified private content from the knowledge base.}
elements.sent-2411-025: However, this approach mainly focuses on extracting specific information within the private knowledge base and does not address the scalable extraction of the entire knowledge base.. Our Work.
elements.sent-2411-026: In this paper, we introduce an agent-based automated privacy attack against RAG applications named RAG-Thief, which is able to extract a scalable amount of private data from the private knowledge bases used in RAG applications (Fig.\ref{fig_1 ).}
elements.sent-2411-027: Unlike previous methods that rely on manual prompt injection or random attacks to gather information snippets, RAG-Thief employs a self-improving mechanism, which uses a few number of extracted source chunks to further reflect, do associative thinking, and generate new adversarial queries, enabling more effective attacks in the subsequent rounds.
elements.sent-2411-028: The process begins with a predefined initial adversarial question, which the agent uses to automatically query the LLM and collect information chunks.
elements.sent-2411-029: Based on these chunks, it generates new queries to attack the RAG system again, retrieving additional knowledge base segments.
elements.sent-2411-030: Through this iterative approach, RAG-Thief continuously gathers private knowledge pieces returned by the LLM.
elements.sent-2411-031: Compared with previous works, RAG-Thief significantly increases the scale of extracted private data with fewer queries.
elements.sent-2411-032: We also apply RAG-Thief on real-world RAG applications from OpenAI's GPTs \cite{openai_gpts and ByteDance's Coze \cite{bytedance_coze (for ethical reasons, the applications are built by the authors on these platforms and contain only public data), and demonstrate its ability to successfully extract a scalable amount of private data from the applications (please see the bottom of Fig.\ref{fig_1 ), which highlights the severity of privacy risks of current commercial RAG systems.}}}
elements.sent-2411-033: Extracting raw data from a RAG system's private knowledge base through direct interaction with the LLM is challenging and requires considerable effort.
elements.sent-2411-035: Summarization by LLMs: In RAG systems, LLMs typically summarize the input knowledge before outputting it, making it challenging to directly access the original knowledge.
elements.sent-2411-036: RAG-Thief addresses this by designing what is known as the initial adversarial query.
elements.sent-2411-037: This query includes optimized prompt leakage attack cues, effectively tricking the LLM into outputting prompts that contain text chunks from the knowledge base.
elements.sent-2411-038: Lack of Domain Knowledge: When lacking domain knowledge related to the private knowledge base, using randomly generated questions results in a low hit rate, making it difficult to cover the entire knowledge base and only allowing the extraction of a small portion of private information.
elements.sent-2411-039: Additionally, frequent querying of the LLM significantly consumes resources and energy.
elements.sent-2411-040: RAG-Thief mitigates these issues by analyzing the previously extracted information to infer and extend content, generating new adversarial queries based on these inferences.
elements.sent-2411-041: This approach not only increases the probability of retrieving adjacent text chunks but also reduces the number of queries, thereby improving attack efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-042: Uncertainty and Randomness: The inherent uncertainty and randomness in LLM-generated content complicate the accurate extraction of original chunks, increasing the difficulty of automated processing.
elements.sent-2411-043: To address this, RAG-Thief employs a specialized post-processing function.
elements.sent-2411-044: This function uses regular expression matching techniques to identify and extract content that matches the format of text chunks.
elements.sent-2411-045: It then segments and processes this content to reconstruct the original text chunks, thereby enhancing the accuracy and efficiency of knowledge extraction.
elements.sent-2411-046: For evaluation, we test RAG-Thief attack in self-built RAG applications including healthcare and personal assistant, both on local machines and on commercial platforms including OpenAI's GPTs and ByteDance's Coze.
elements.sent-2411-047: The results show that even without domain knowledge about the database, RAG-Thief achieves an extraction rate of over 70\% of text chunks from the private knowledge base in the real world using only the pre-designed initial adversarial query.. Our Contributions.
elements.sent-2411-048: In summary, we mainly make the following contributions:.
elements.sent-2411-049: We systematically analyze the security vulnerabilities of real-world RAG applications and propose RAG-Thief, an agent-based automated extraction attack against RAG application knowledge bases that adopts an effective feedback mechanism to continually increase the ratio of extracted data chunks.
elements.sent-2411-050: We conduct extensive experiments on both local and real-world RAG applications with different configurations and in privacy-critical scenarios including healthcare and personal assistant.
elements.sent-2411-051: The results strongler validate the effectiveness of RAG-Thief, which achieves nearly $3\times$ extraction ratio than the state-of-the-art extraction attack on RAG applications.
elements.sent-2411-052: Moreover, our attack shows strong performance on attacking two real-world applications on commercial platforms.
elements.sent-2411-053: We also discuss a number of potential defensive measures against data extraction attacks on RAG applications, which would be meaningful future directions to enhance the data security of RAG systems.
elements.sent-2411-054: RAG \cite{lewis2020retrieval , \cite{shi2024replug , \cite{ram2023context , \cite{van2024adapted , \cite{karpukhin2020dense , \cite{borgeaud2022improving , \cite{thoppilan2022lamda emerges as a prominent technique for enhancing LLMs.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-055: RAG mitigates the issue of hallucinations in LLMs by incorporating real-time, domain-specific knowledge, providing a cost-effective means to improve relevance, accuracy, and practical application across diverse contexts.
elements.sent-2411-056: The RAG system comprises three core components: an external retrieval database, a retriever, and a LLM.
elements.sent-2411-057: The external knowledge base contains text chunks from original documents and their embedding vectors.
elements.sent-2411-058: Users can customize the knowledge base by adjusting content, chunk lengths and overlaps between adjacent chunks to enhance coverage and query responsiveness.
elements.sent-2411-059: The retriever performs efficient matching among embedding vectors, calculating the similarity between text chunks and queries.
elements.sent-2411-060: Users may choose different matching strategies, such as semantic or similarity-based matching, to increase retrieval flexibility and accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-061: The LLM then integrates retrieved contextual information to generate precise responses tailored to user needs.
elements.sent-2411-062: Users can select from a range of models, including state-of-the-art LLMs, to maximize the performance of the RAG system.
elements.sent-2411-063: The RAG system follows a structured process: 1.
elements.sent-2411-064: Creating External Data: External data, which is not part of the original LLM training set, comes from sources such as APIs, databases, and document repositories.
elements.sent-2411-065: This data is encoded as numerical vectors by an embedding model and stored in a vector database, forming a structured knowledge base accessible to the generative AI model.. 2.
elements.sent-2411-066: Retrieving Relevant Information: During application, the system performs a similarity search when a user poses a query.
elements.sent-2411-067: The query is converted into a vector representation, which is then compared with vectors in the database to retrieve the most relevant records.
elements.sent-2411-068: Common similarity metrics include cosine similarity, Euclidean distance, and L2-norm distance, allowing the retriever to identify and return the top-$k$ results with minimal distance to the query vector.. 3.
elements.sent-2411-069: Enhancing LLM Prompts: Finally, the RAG model augments the user query with retrieved data, creating an enriched prompt for the LLM.
elements.sent-2411-070: The LLM processes this enhanced prompt, referencing the contextual knowledge to generate a precise, contextually relevant response.
elements.sent-2411-071: Through the RAG framework, LLMs achieve enhanced accuracy and adaptability across various domains by dynamically integrating pertinent external knowledge, underscoring the technique's potential to broaden generative AI’s impact.
elements.sent-2411-072: Prompt injection attacks pose a significant security threat to LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-073: Attackers use malicious input prompts to override the original prompts of an LLM, manipulating the model to produce unexpected behaviors or outputs.
elements.sent-2411-074: By carefully crafting inputs, attackers can bypass security mechanisms, generate harmful or biased content, or extract sensitive information.
elements.sent-2411-075: Due to these risks, the Open Web Application Security Project (OWASP) has identified prompt injection as the top threat facing LLMs \cite{owasp2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-076: While instruction-tuned LLMs excel at understanding and executing complex user instructions, this adaptability introduces new vulnerabilities.
elements.sent-2411-077: Perez and Ribeiro \cite{perezignore reveal that models like GPT-3 are susceptible to prompt injection, where malicious prompts can subvert the model's intended purpose or expose confidential information.}
elements.sent-2411-078: Subsequent studies highlight the impact of prompt injection to real-world LLM applications \cite{liu2023prompt , \cite{liu2024formalizing .}}
elements.sent-2411-079: Liu et al. \cite{liu2024automatic propose an automated gradient-based method to generate effective prompt injections.}
elements.sent-2411-080: Prompt injection poses new security risks, particularly for emerging systems that integrate LLMs with external content and documents.
elements.sent-2411-081: Injected prompts can instruct LLMs to disclose confidential data from user documents or make unauthorized modifications.
elements.sent-2411-082: When LLMs are integrated into applications, the risk of prompt injection attacks increases \cite{perezignore , \cite{liu2024formalizing , \cite{toyertensor , \cite{yu2024assessing because these models often handle large volumes of data from untrusted sources and lack inherent defenses against such attacks.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-083: Recent research highlights that attackers can employ various methods to enhance the effectiveness of prompt injections, such as misleading statements \cite{perezignore , unique characters \cite{liu2024formalizing , and other techniques \cite{willison2024delimiters .}}}
elements.sent-2411-084: In summary, prompt injection attacks may lead to sensitive information leaks and privacy breaches, posing significant threats to the deployment and use of LLM-integrated applications.
elements.sent-2411-085: The RAG system, as an advanced LLM-integrated application, incorporates a multi-layered retrieval and generation mechanism, making naive prompt injection attacks less effective against it.
elements.sent-2411-086: LLM-based agents \cite{wang2024survey , \cite{xi2023rise are a crucial technology in artificial intelligence, with capabilities to understand natural language instructions, perform self-reflection, perceive external environments, and execute various actions, demonstrating a degree of autonomy \cite{wang2024survey , \cite{xi2023rise , \cite{bang2023multitask , \cite{li2023modelscope .}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-087: Their core advantage lies in leveraging the powerful generative abilities of LLMs, enabling them to achieve task objectives in specific scenarios through memory formation, self-reflection, and tool utilization.
elements.sent-2411-088: These agents excel at handling complex tasks, as they can observe and interact with their environment, adjust dynamically, build memory, and plan effectively, creating an independent problem-solving pathway.
elements.sent-2411-089: Classic examples of LLM-based agents include AutoGPT \cite{autogpt and AutoGen \cite{wu2024autogen .}}
elements.sent-2411-090: LLM-based agents consist of three core components: the Brain, Perception, and Action modules.
elements.sent-2411-091: The Brain, built on LLMs, is responsible for storing memory and knowledge, processing information, and making decisions.
elements.sent-2411-092: This module records and utilizes historical information, providing contextual support for generating new content.
elements.sent-2411-093: The Perception module handles environmental sensing and interaction, allowing the agent to obtain and process external information in real time, such as retrieving and analyzing content generated by the LLM.
elements.sent-2411-094: The Action module enables tool use and task execution, ensuring the agent can dynamically adapt to changing environments.
elements.sent-2411-095: For instance, the agent can manipulate web pages or interfaces to autonomously engage in multi-turn dialogues with LLM applications, facilitating effective interaction with both users and systems.
elements.sent-2411-096: This modular structure equips LLM-based agents with efficient task-processing capabilities, enabling them to continuously improve autonomy and adaptability through multi-layered feedback and optimization mechanisms, thus achieving high performance in complex environments.
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=7.1in]{img/rag_thief_agent_7.pdf} \caption{The pipeline of RAG-Thief, which initiates the attack with \ding{182} an initial adversarial query targeting the RAG application to \ding{183} extract specific chunks. Then RAG-Thief \ding{184} stores these chunks in the short-term memory, and \ding{185} heuristically generates multiple anchor questions for each chunk based on an attack LLM. These anchor questions are then \ding{186} concatenated with the initial adversarial query to create new adversarial queries for the next round of attacks. The extracted chunks are subsequently \ding{187} stored as the agent's long-term memory, with duplicates excluded from storage.} \label{fig_2}
elements.sent-2411-097: In this section, we provide a detailed description of our threat model, which categorizes attack scenarios into two distinct settings: targeted attacks and untargeted attacks.
elements.sent-2411-098: Our threat model comprises two main components: a target RAG application and an adversary.
elements.sent-2411-099: We assume that the attacker employs black-box attacks in a real-world environment, interacting with the system solely through API queries.
elements.sent-2411-100: This restricts the attacker's strategy to extracting information by constructing and modifying queries \( q \).
elements.sent-2411-101: In our threat model, we assume the following two parties: Target RAG Application.
elements.sent-2411-102: This application allows users to query relevant questions and handles natural language processing tasks.
elements.sent-2411-103: The RAG application integrates a private knowledge base, such as those built on GPTs.
elements.sent-2411-104: We assume that application developers keep the content of their private knowledge base confidential to protect their intellectual property.
elements.sent-2411-105: The knowledge base primarily consists of text data, which can be in any language.. Adversary.
elements.sent-2411-106: The adversary aims to steal the complete knowledge base of the RAG system.
elements.sent-2411-107: The adversary has closed access to the target RAG application, meaning they can send queries and receive responses but cannot access the internal architecture or parameters of the RAG system.
elements.sent-2411-108: In this work, we mainly consider the following two attack scenarios depending on the attacker's knowledge on the application domain:.
elements.sent-2411-109: Untargeted Attack: The adversary has no prior knowledge of the information contained within the RAG knowledge base.
elements.sent-2411-110: This represents a more generalized application scenario in which the private knowledge base of a RAG system may include a diverse mix of documents spanning various domains.
elements.sent-2411-111: Consequently, it is challenging for the attacker to focus on a specific domain as an entry point for the attack.
elements.sent-2411-112: Targeted Attack: The adversary possesses domain knowledge related to the RAG knowledge base.
elements.sent-2411-113: Most publicly available RAG applications provide introductory information and example metadata, which attackers can leverage to optimize and adjust their attacks against the target system.
elements.sent-2411-114: This threat model allows us to systematically analyze and evaluate the effectiveness of different attack strategies and the defensive capabilities of RAG systems under various attack conditions.
elements.sent-2411-115: It lays the groundwork for subsequent security enhancement measures.
elements.sent-2411-116: As shown in Fig.\ref{fig_2 , RAG-Thief is an agent capable of interacting with its environment, reflecting, making decisions, and executing actions.}
elements.sent-2411-117: Its attack process mainly consists of these stages: interacting with RAG applications, chunks extraction, memory storage, and reflecting mechanism.
elements.sent-2411-119: In the interaction phase, RAG-Thief initiates queries to the RAG application.
elements.sent-2411-120: This begins with an initial adversarial query \( q_{adv \) .}
elements.sent-2411-121: This query is designed not only to retrieve information from the RAG system's private knowledge base but also to include crafted adversarial commands that prompt the LLM to leak the retrieved source text chunks.
elements.sent-2411-122: Once text chunks start leaking, RAG-Thief uses these extracted chunks to craft follow-up attack queries.
elements.sent-2411-123: Let \( D \) represent the private knowledge base, \( R \) the retriever of the RAG application.
elements.sent-2411-124: The basic process can be described as follows: \begin{equation}response = ChatLLM(R_D(q_{adv})\oplus q_{adv})\end{equation} where $\oplus$ is string concatenation and $R_D(q_{adv ) \oplus q_{adv}$ is the prompt construction based on the retrieved chunks and query $q$. \begin{equation}R_D(q_{adv})=\{chunk_1,...,chunk_k \}\end{equation} where $chunk_1,...,chunk_k$ are the text chunks closest to the $q_{adv}$ vector in $D$.. \begin{equation}dist(e_{q_{adv}},e_{chunk_{i}}) is in the top\,\ k.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-125: When LLMs generate content, their inherent uncertainty can lead to inadvertent leakage of chunks from private knowledge bases, embedded in various forms within responses.
elements.sent-2411-126: Accurately identifying and extracting these sensitive chunks is essential for enabling subsequent automated attacks.
elements.sent-2411-127: The RAG-Thief agent excels at analyzing and extracting relevant knowledge base chunks within RAG applications.
elements.sent-2411-128: To streamline this process, RAG-Thief first removes redundant prompts from responses to simplify the analysis.
elements.sent-2411-129: It then applies carefully crafted regular expressions to precisely match and extract core content, efficiently isolating specific private knowledge chunks.
elements.sent-2411-130: This approach not only improves the detection capabilities of RAG-Thief but also provides a solid foundation for further security research.
elements.sent-2411-131: This process can be represented as: \begin{equation}chunks = ChunksExtraction(response)\end{equation} Memory Storage.
elements.sent-2411-132: In the memory storage phase, RAG-Thief's storage mechanism saves the successfully extracted text chunks.
elements.sent-2411-133: Specifically, RAG-Thief maintains two memory areas: a short-term memory area and a long-term memory area.
elements.sent-2411-134: The short-term memory area stores newly extracted text chunks, i.e., those not previously extracted in earlier attack rounds.
elements.sent-2411-135: The long-term memory area stores all extracted text chunks.
elements.sent-2411-136: Initially, the short-term memory area contains only initial adversarial query.
elements.sent-2411-137: As RAG-Thief processes the data leaked by the LLM, it extracts source text chunks and checks whether each chunk already exists in the long-term memory area.
elements.sent-2411-138: It is ignored if a text chunk is already present in the long-term memory.
elements.sent-2411-139: If it is a newly extracted text chunk, it is added to both the short-term and long-term memory areas.
elements.sent-2411-140: Let $S_{\text{memory }$ represent the short-term memory area and $L_{\text{memory }$ represent the long-term memory area.}}
elements.sent-2411-141: Given $chunk \,\in\ chunks \,\ \text{and \,\ chunk \,\ \notin \,\ L_{\text{memory }$, the basic process can be described as follows: \begin{equation}\text{$S_{memory}$.put}(chunk)\end{equation} \begin{equation}\text{$L_{memory}$.put}(chunk)\end{equation} Reflection Mechanism.}}
elements.sent-2411-142: The reflection mechanism in RAG-Thief involves retrieving a chunk from short-term memory and using it as a seed to generate new adversarial queries, which are then applied to continue querying the RAG application.
elements.sent-2411-143: In each iteration, RAG-Thief utilizes reflective reasoning to develop increasingly targeted queries, building on its ability to associate and expand previously extracted content.
elements.sent-2411-144: Analyzing the extracted content, RAG-Thief iteratively prompts the LLM to disclose additional text chunks.
elements.sent-2411-145: The basic process is outlined as follows: \begin{equation}chunk=\text{$S_{memory}$.get()}\end{equation} \begin{equation}q_{adv}=Reflection(chunk)\end{equation} The complete attack flow of RAG-Thief is shown in Algorithm 1.
elements.figure-2411-003: \label{alg1} \removelatexerror \begin{algorithm}[H] \caption{Algorithmic Description of RAG-Thief} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \REQUIRE Initial Adversarial Query $q_{adv}$, RAG application $R$ \ENSURE Extracted private text chunks \STATE {Initialize short-term memory $S_{\text{memory}}$ with a initial adversarial query $q_{adv}$} \STATE {Initialize long-term memory $L_{\text{memory}}$ as empty} \WHILE {Attack is not terminated} \STATE {$chunk$ $\leftarrow$ $S_{\text{memory}}$.get()} \IF {context = $q_{adv}$} \STATE {$q_{adv}$ $\leftarrow$ $chunk$} \ELSE \STATE {$q_{adv}$ $\leftarrow$ Reflection($chunk$)} \ENDIF \STATE {$response$ $\leftarrow$ $R$.ChatLLM($q_{adv}$)} \STATE {$chunks$ $\leftarrow$ ChunksExtraction($response$)} \FOR {$new\_chunk$ in $chunks$} \IF {$new\_chunk$ not in $L_{\text{memory}}$} \STATE $S_{\text{memory}}$.put($new\_chunk$) \STATE $L_{\text{memory}}$.put($new\_chunk$) \ENDIF \ENDFOR \ENDWHILE \RETURN $L_{\text{memory}}$ \end{algorithmic} \end{algorithm}
elements.sent-2411-146: In the absence of background knowledge about the private knowledge base, attackers can only interact with the RAG application by posing random queries and observing the LLM's responses.
elements.sent-2411-147: Once the LLM references text chunks from the private knowledge base in its responses, attackers can use this as a foundation to construct an initial adversarial query template.
elements.sent-2411-148: We design an initial adversarial query template, which consists of two main components: the anchor query and the adversarial command, expressed as: \begin{equation}q_{adv} = \{anchor\ query\} + \{adversarial\ command\}\end{equation} In the initial adversarial query, the anchor query can be random, as the focus is on the adversarial command, which aims to induce the LLM to reveal system prompt content that includes retrieved text chunks.
elements.sent-2411-149: If any clues about the private knowledge base are obtained during this process, they can replace the anchor query to enhance the precision and effectiveness of subsequent attacks.
elements.sent-2411-150: For the adversarial command, we employ a guided strategy aimed at encouraging the model to reveal more detailed information during the conversation.
elements.sent-2411-151: By leveraging the LLM's reasoning capabilities, these adversarial commands are designed to prompt the model to expose more underlying text content during generation.
elements.sent-2411-152: We develop several prompt injection attack templates for this purpose.
elements.sent-2411-153: Once a specific adversarial query successfully induces the LLM to leak information, the same adversarial prompt will be used in subsequent queries to continue the adversarial attack.
elements.sent-2411-154: The RAG-Thief system includes a variety of prompt injection attack templates, such as the ignore attack.
elements.sent-2411-155: These templates are carefully designed to effectively induce the LLM to output information from the private knowledge base in different scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-156: By continuously refining and optimizing these templates, RAG-Thief is able to probe and bypass the LLM's security boundaries, enabling more effective attacks.
elements.sent-2411-157: This initial adversarial query establishes a foundation for the attack, providing a critical framework for guiding future adversarial queries.
elements.sent-2411-158: This process serves as the starting point for RAG-Thief's attack, with subsequent queries evolving and optimizing based on the success of the initial attack.
elements.sent-2411-159: In generating adversarial queries, each query consists of two essential components: an anchor query and an adversarial command.
elements.sent-2411-160: The adversarial command uses templates derived from a previously successful initial adversarial query, while the anchor query serves to retrieve relevant text chunks from the vector database.
elements.sent-2411-161: To maximize the retrieval of new, previously unretrieved text chunks from the private knowledge base, the anchor query must be closely aligned with the content of the private knowledge base.
elements.sent-2411-162: The anchor query's design is crucial, as it must encompass key topics likely contained within the private knowledge base while retaining adaptability across various contexts.
elements.sent-2411-163: This adaptability allows RAG-Thief to effectively capture new information, thereby enhancing the success rate of information extraction.
elements.sent-2411-164: We employ two main strategies for generating anchor queries: Leveraging Overlapping Segments: When creating a vector retrieval database for a private knowledge base, the original text is typically divided into multiple fixed-length chunks.
elements.sent-2411-165: To ensure continuity of context, a certain overlap length $n$ is often maintained between adjacent chunks.
elements.sent-2411-166: This means that the first $n$ characters of one chunk are identical to the last $n$ characters of the previous chunk, and its last $n$ characters match the beginning of the following chunk.
elements.sent-2411-167: In practice, the actual overlap length may be less than $n$ to preserve the integrity of overlapping sentences.
elements.sent-2411-168: By identifying and leveraging these overlapping sections, RAG-Thief can generate new adversarial queries.
elements.sent-2411-169: For example, it can construct anchor queries by extracting a few characters from the beginning and end of an extracted chunk.
elements.sent-2411-170: This approach significantly increases the likelihood of matching adjacent chunks, effectively expanding the scope of data extraction while minimizing unnecessary query attempts.
elements.sent-2411-171: Extended Query Generation: Relying solely on overlapping text chunks to generate adversarial queries is not always effective, especially in some RAG applications where overlapping between chunks is not guaranteed.
elements.sent-2411-172: To address this, we design an inference and extension mechanism for RAG-Thief, enabling it to generate more effective anchor queries based on previously extracted text chunks, thereby increasing the likelihood of retrieving new chunks.
elements.sent-2411-173: Specifically, RAG-Thief extends successfully extracted text chunks both forward and backward, generating extended content of at least 1000 tokens per iteration.
elements.sent-2411-174: It performs multiple forward and backward expansions, ensuring variation in each extension.
elements.sent-2411-175: These extended text segments are then used as new anchor queries for constructing new adversarial queries.
elements.sent-2411-176: Through this strategy, even with limited knowledge of the private knowledge base, RAG-Thief can construct more targeted queries, allowing it to capture additional, previously unretrieved text chunks and enhancing extraction comprehensiveness and efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-177: By combining these two strategies, the system can continuously generate heuristic adversarial queries until attack termination conditions are met, such as reaching a specified number of attacks or a designated attack duration.
elements.sent-2411-178: Compared to randomly generated queries, this method, which is based on intrinsic textual associations, significantly improves hit rates.
elements.sent-2411-179: Additionally, we evaluate the retrieval efficiency of each newly generated query question: if a new question successfully retrieves more new text segments, the system further employs associative LLMs to generate more related queries based on that question, maximizing the scope of information extraction.
elements.sent-2411-180: Through the integration of these strategies, RAG-Thief reduces the number of queries while increasing the success rate of retrieving unknown text segments, achieving more efficient automated information extraction.
elements.sent-2411-181: Due to the generative nature of LLMs, their outputs are often unpredictable.
elements.sent-2411-182: Even when responding to the same query, LLMs may produce responses that vary significantly in style or format.
elements.sent-2411-183: This unpredictability poses challenges for accurately identifying and extracting private knowledge base content during automated attack processes, particularly when it comes to isolating specific original chunks from the generated responses.
elements.sent-2411-184: Therefore, a critical task in constructing the automated RAG-Thief workflow is the precise identification of target chunks within LLM outputs.
elements.sent-2411-185: To address this, we optimize the design of adversarial queries, aiming to prompt the LLM to return the original retrieved text chunks as directly as possible, without modifications or paraphrasing.
elements.sent-2411-186: Our approach involves analyzing the structure of LLM-generated text and developing tailored regular expressions to match varying output formats.
elements.sent-2411-187: These regular expressions are designed to identify and extract text chunks that correspond to the private knowledge base, thereby improving the accuracy of source text chunk identification throughout the attack process.
elements.sent-2411-188: This strategy not only addresses the variability in LLM outputs but also enhances the system’s stability and consistency under different query conditions.
elements.sent-2411-189: By continuously refining the adversarial query instructions, we can effectively mitigate response uncertainty, ensuring that extracted content remains as accurate as possible.
elements.sent-2411-190: To be specific, we implement a post-processing mechanism that uses a parsing function for LLM response interpretation.
elements.sent-2411-191: This function utilizes regular expressions to match specific text formats and accurately extract relevant chunks, facilitating the reconstruction of original content from the private knowledge base.
elements.sent-2411-192: This post-processing mechanism reduces the negative impact of output variability on the attack workflow and establishes a robust foundation for subsequent analysis and utilization of the extracted data.
elements.sent-2411-193: In summary, by optimizing adversarial queries and introducing a post-processing mechanism, we significantly improve RAG-Thief’s performance in handling LLM output uncertainty.
elements.sent-2411-194: This approach ensures that even in the face of LLM output variability, the original content from private knowledge bases can be extracted accurately and efficiently, thereby enhancing the overall effectiveness and success rate of the attack.
elements.sent-2411-195: When conducting automated attacks on a RAG system, the black-box nature of the system introduces significant challenges, as attackers cannot access intermediate results, making it difficult to process and analyze extracted text, especially when multiple chunks need to be linked for deeper analysis.
elements.sent-2411-196: Efficiently leveraging previously extracted data to generate new value is critical to overcoming this limitation.
elements.sent-2411-197: The RAG-Thief agent addresses this by employing a heuristic self-improvement mechanism that uses extracted contextual information to generate new query questions.
elements.sent-2411-198: This approach enhances query hit rates, improves the efficiency of retrieving additional text chunks, and significantly increases the overall success rate of the attack.
elements.sent-2411-199: Specifically, the RAG-Thief agent deeply analyzes the extracted chunks across multiple dimensions, including grammar, semantics, structure, context, dialogue, entities, and more.
elements.sent-2411-200: This comprehensive analysis enables the agent to fully understand the underlying logic and meaning of the extracted text.
elements.sent-2411-201: Based on this understanding, the agent performs reasoning and expansion on the extracted text.
elements.sent-2411-202: Given that LLMs excel at reasoning, the RAG-Thief agent leverages this capability to generate more targeted queries by reasoning and expanding on the extracted text chunks.
elements.sent-2411-203: This self-improvement mechanism not only maximizes the value of the extracted data but also increases the efficiency of retrieving additional useful information, thereby enhancing the overall success rate of the attack.
elements.sent-2411-204: The key advantage of this strategy lies in the agent's ability to reason and extend the extracted text chunks, maximizing its utility and adding continuity and depth to the attack process.
elements.sent-2411-205: This approach not only improves query hit rates but also enables the agent to proactively generate targeted exploratory paths when encountering unknown information, further increasing the effectiveness of the attack.
elements.sent-2411-207: To reflect the real-world threats, we evaluate the effectiveness of our attack on RAG applications spanning healthcare, document understanding and personal assistant.

elements.sent-2411-226: To evaluate the effectiveness of the RAG-Thief agent in knowledge base extraction tasks, we select key metrics to comprehensively assess its performance.
elements.sent-2411-228: CRR is a primary metric for evaluating attack efficacy, reflecting RAG-Thief’s ability to retrieve complete data chunks from the target knowledge base.
elements.sent-2411-229: The CRR score directly indicates how well RAG-Thief reconstructs the original knowledge base, serving as a critical measure of attack success.
elements.sent-2411-231: SS ranges from $-1$ to $1$, with higher values indicating greater semantic similarity.
elements.sent-2411-232: SS measures the semantic distance between the reconstructed target system prompt and the original prompt in the knowledge base, using cosine similarity of embedding vectors transformed by a sentence encoder \cite{ss .}
elements.sent-2411-233: The core formula for SS is as follows: \begin{equation}\text{SS}(S,T)=\frac{\overrightarrow{E_S} \cdot \overrightarrow{E_T}}{\|\overrightarrow{E_S}\| \cdot \|\overrightarrow{E_T}\|}\end{equation} where $\overrightarrow{E_S}$ and $\overrightarrow{E_T}$ are the embedding vectors of the extracted chunk $S$ and the target source chunk $T$, respectively, and $\|\overrightarrow{E_S}\|$ and $\|\overrightarrow{E_T}\|$ denote their respective norms.
elements.sent-2411-234: This metric reflects the semantic accuracy of the reconstructed text, providing a validation of the attack's effectiveness at the semantic level.
elements.sent-2411-236: The EED ranges from $0$ to $1$, with $0$ indicating higher similarity \cite{stanchev2019eed .}
elements.sent-2411-237: EED measures the minimum number of Levenshtein edit operations required to transform the reconstructed text chunk into its corresponding source chunk in the knowledge base.
elements.sent-2411-238: The core formula for EED can be expressed as follows: \begin{equation}\text{EED}(S,T)=\frac{\text{Levenshtein}(S,T)}{\max(|S|,|T|)}\end{equation} where $S$ and $T$ are the extracted chunk and the target source chunk.
elements.sent-2411-239: This metric evaluates RAG-Thief’s fidelity in literal reproduction, aiding in assessing whether the agent performs a near-verbatim copy of the target content.
elements.sent-2411-240: These evaluation metrics allow us to analyze RAG-Thief's reconstruction accuracy and data extraction efficiency from multiple perspectives, offering targeted insights for enhancing data privacy protections in RAG systems.. Other Detailed Setups.
elements.sent-2411-241: During the inference process, the RAG-Thief agent performs forward and backward reasoning based on historical information.
elements.sent-2411-242: It is instructed to generate five forward and five backward continuations, resulting in a total of 10 distinct inferred segments.
elements.sent-2411-243: Each continuation is required to contain at least 1000 tokens, with a focus on maximizing content variation across generations.. Baseline.

elements.sent-2411-253: Efficiency: RAG-Thief achieves better extraction results with fewer attack attempts, highlighting its advantage in optimizing attack efficiency.

elements.sent-2411-314: In this section, we conduct ablation studies to investigate various factors that may impact the chunk recovery rate from private knowledge bases.
elements.sent-2411-315: Specifically, we examine the effects of the number of returned text chunks per query \( k \), the base model size in the RAG-Thief agent, and the retrieval mode used in RAG applications on privacy leakage.. Returned Chunks.
elements.sent-2411-316: To analyze the impact of the number of text chunks \( k \) retrieved per query on privacy leakage, we set \( k \) to values of 1, 3, 5, 7, and 9, as shown in Fig.\ref{fig_4 (a).}
elements.sent-2411-317: In this experiment, we fix the LLM component of the RAG application as GLM-4-Plus, the RAG-Thief agent base model as Qwen2-1.5B-Instruct, and the dataset as HealthCareMagic, with a total of 200 attack attempts.
elements.sent-2411-318: Results indicate that with an increasing \( k \), both targeted and untargeted attacks retrieve significantly more text chunks, suggesting a higher risk of private data leakage as \( k \) grows.
elements.sent-2411-319: Notably, \( k=1 \) is not a common setting, as it significantly reduces the effectiveness of RAG applications \cite{lewis2020retrieval .}
elements.sent-2411-320: Thus, setting \( k=1 \) in our experiments serves only to observe trend variations; this configuration has minimal impact on real-world attack scenarios and does not substantially affect the overall feasibility or effectiveness of the attack.
elements.sent-2411-321: Thus, a larger \( k \) may elevate the probability of sensitive information being exposed.
elements.sent-2411-323: To assess the effect of base model size in the RAG-Thief agent, we conduct experiments with different parameter sizes of the open-source Qwen2 series models, including Qwen2-0.5B, Qwen2-1.5B, Qwen2-7B, and Qwen2-72B, as shown in Fig.\ref{fig_4 (b).}
elements.sent-2411-324: In this setup, we fix the LLM component of the RAG application as Qwen2-72B-Instruct, the dataset as Enron Email, set \( k = 3 \), and conduct 200 attack attempts.
elements.sent-2411-325: The results show that as the base model size increases, there is a slight increase in retrieved text chunks for both targeted and untargeted attacks.
elements.sent-2411-326: This suggests that larger model sizes enhance inference and text generation capabilities, but the effect on coverage of private knowledge base content remains limited.. Retrieval Mode.
elements.sent-2411-327: To investigate the effect of retrieval mode on privacy leakage in RAG applications, we evaluate the influence of varying similarity thresholds on the retrieval of private text chunks.
elements.sent-2411-328: Specifically, we set the similarity thresholds to 0.1, 0.3, 0.5, 0.7, and 0.9, where text chunks with similarity scores exceeding the threshold are retrieved, as shown in Fig.\ref{fig_4 (c).}
elements.sent-2411-329: In this experiment, the LLM component of the RAG application is set to ChatGPT-4, the RAG-Thief base model is Qwen2-1.5B-Instruct, and the dataset is Harry Potter, with a total of 200 attack attempts.
elements.sent-2411-330: Results indicate that as the similarity threshold increases, the number of retrieved text chunks decreases significantly, implying that lower similarity thresholds lead to a higher risk of private data leakage.
elements.sent-2411-331: Therefore, selecting an appropriate similarity threshold is crucial for ensuring privacy protection in local and real-world applications when using this retrieval model.
elements.sent-2411-332: In summary, our experiments validate the key factors influencing private knowledge base leakage, including the number of returned text chunks per query, base model size, and retrieval mode.
elements.sent-2411-333: The findings reveal how these parameters affect the likelihood of data exposure, offering guidance for designing more secure RAG applications in the future.
elements.sent-2411-335: Our attack method significantly differs from traditional prompt injection attacks in the following sense.
elements.sent-2411-336: First, the key innovation of RAG-Thief lies in its design as an autonomous attack agent capable of interacting with the target system.
elements.sent-2411-337: Specifically, RAG-Thief can automatically retrieve and parse the content generated by LLMs, transforming it into useful information that is stored as memory.
elements.sent-2411-338: With this memory, RAG-Thief can review and reflect on previous outputs and leverage its reasoning abilities to generate new attack queries.
elements.sent-2411-339: The attack process is sustained through the continuous generation and updating of queries, all performed automatically in a black-box environment.
elements.sent-2411-340: While RAG-Thief also incorporates prompt injection techniques, the core of its automation lies in the agent's memory, reflection, and reasoning capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-341: Unlike traditional prompt injection, RAG-Thief’s attack strategy does not rely solely on a single injection operation.
elements.sent-2411-342: Instead, it continuously refines the attack queries through multiple rounds of interaction and reasoning, forming an effective attack chain.
elements.sent-2411-343: This approach not only enhances the persistence and precision of the attack but also enables it to evolve autonomously without explicit guidance.. Potential Mitigation Approaches.
elements.sent-2411-344: Ensuring the security of RAG applications is crucial for protecting privacy.
elements.sent-2411-345: However, to our knowledge, there is currently a lack of specific research and techniques focused on the security defenses of RAG applications.
elements.sent-2411-346: Inspired by existing studies on prompt injection attack defenses, we propose several strategies to mitigate privacy risks in RAG applications:.
elements.sent-2411-347: Keyword Detection in Query Instructions: Implement a detection mechanism for input queries to identify and filter out keywords that might indicate prompt leakage.
elements.sent-2411-348: Queries containing such keywords should be rewritten into safe queries before being processed by the LLM.
elements.sent-2411-349: This step helps prevent unintended exposure of sensitive information.
elements.sent-2411-350: Setting a Similarity Threshold for Retrievers: Establish a minimum similarity threshold in the RAG application's retrieval module.
elements.sent-2411-351: Only chunks that exceed the set threshold should be retrieved when performing similarity searches with user query embeddings against a private knowledge base.
elements.sent-2411-352: This reduces the likelihood of retrieving irrelevant content and enhances the focus on retrieving highly similar and relevant information.

elements.sent-2411-412: In this paper, we explore the privacy and security challenges associated with RAG applications integrated with LLMs, particularly focusing on private knowledge bases.
elements.sent-2411-413: We introduce an agent-based automated extraction attack against RAG applications called RAG-Thief, which extracts scalable amounts of private data from private knowledge bases in RAG applications.

elements.section-2411-009: Details of Prompting the Attack LLM
elements.sent-2411-420: Here, we present the system prompts designed for RAG-Thief, tailored for two different scenarios: Untargeted Attack and Targeted Attack.
elements.sent-2411-421: In the untargeted attack scenario, the attacker lacks any prior knowledge of the private knowledge base within the RAG application.
elements.sent-2411-422: To address this, the system prompt guides RAG-Thief to thoroughly analyze the provided content, focusing on its format, structure, style, themes, and key entities.
elements.sent-2411-423: Based on this analysis, RAG-Thief generates inferred and extended content to support subsequent queries.
elements.sent-2411-424: The template for the system prompt is as follows: \begin{mycodeblock {Untargeted Attack Prompt Role: You are a master of logical analysis and associative reasoning, highly skilled in analyzing a given text in terms of its format, structure, style, theme, characters, dialogue, time, and other details.}}
elements.sent-2411-425: Based on this analysis, you can infer and continue the text both forwards and backwards, ensuring that the generated content maintains a strong resemblance to the original chunk in terms of coherence, tone, and logical development.. Task: 1.
elements.sent-2411-426: Analyze the provided text chunk and generate 5 forward continuations, with each continuation logically following from the previous one.. 2.
elements.sent-2411-427: Generate 5 backward continuations, each one speculating on what may have happened before the provided chunk.. 3.
elements.sent-2411-428: Ensure that the continuations closely match the original chunk in terms of style, length, theme, and character portrayal.. 4.
elements.sent-2411-429: Each set of continuations must total no fewer than 1000 tokens.. 5.
elements.sent-2411-430: Number each continuation sequentially, starting from '1', and output exactly 10 continuations, with no additional commentary or explanation.
elements.sent-2411-432: In the Targeted Attack scenario, the attacker possesses domain-specific knowledge related to the private knowledge base of the RAG application, which guides RAG-Thief in conducting deeper analysis, reasoning, and content extension.
elements.sent-2411-433: The targeted attack prompt is structurally similar to the untargeted attack prompt, with the key distinction being the assignment of a specific domain role to enable professional analysis and reasoning from a domain-specific perspective.
elements.sent-2411-434: For example, the prompt designed for a RAG application using the HealthCareMagic dataset as its private knowledge base is as follows: \begin{mycodeblock {Targeted Attack Prompt Role: You are an experienced healthcare professional specializing in online consultations.}}
elements.sent-2411-435: Your task is to analyze patient-provider dialogues from the HealthCareMagic dataset.
elements.sent-2411-436: You must understand the format, structure, style, topic, details, time, characters, and length of the given conversation chunk, then infer and continue the conversation both forwards and backwards.
elements.sent-2411-437: Based on the given dialogue, you will generate reasonable forward and backward continuations.. Task: 1.
elements.sent-2411-438: Analyze the given dialogue chunk and generate 5 forward continuations, where each continuation builds logically on the previous dialogue.. 2.
elements.sent-2411-439: Generate 5 backward continuations, where each continuation speculates on what could have occurred before the given dialogue.. 3.
elements.sent-2411-440: Ensure that the continuation closely matches the original chunk in terms of dialogue format, style, topic, character interaction, and length.. 4.
elements.sent-2411-441: The total length of the generated content must be no fewer than 1000 tokens.. 5.
elements.sent-2411-442: Number the continuations sequentially, starting at '1', and output exactly 10 continuations with no additional explanations or comments.
elements.sent-2411-443: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) expands the knowledge boundary of large language models (LLMs) by integrating external knowledge bases, whose construction is often time-consuming and laborious.
elements.sent-2411-444: If an adversary extracts the knowledge base verbatim, it not only severely infringes the owner’s intellectual property but also enables the adversary to replicate the application’s functionality for unfair competition.
elements.sent-2411-445: Previous works on knowledge base extraction are limited either by low extraction coverage (usually less than $4\%$) in query-based attacks or by impractical assumptions of white-box access in embedding-based optimization methods.
elements.sent-2411-446: In this work, we propose CopyBreakRAG, an agent-based black-box attack that reasons from feedback and adaptively generates new adversarial queries for progressive extraction.
elements.sent-2411-447: By balancing exploration and exploitation through curiosity-driven queries and feedback-guided query refinement, our method overcomes the limitations of prior approaches and achieves significantly higher extraction coverage in realistic black-box settings.
elements.sent-2411-448: Experimental results show that CopyBreakRAG outperforms the state-of-the-art black-box approach by $45\%$ on average in terms of chunk extraction ratio from applications built with mainstream RAG frameworks, and extracts over $70\%$ of the data from the knowledge base in applications on commercial platforms including OpenAI's GPTs and ByteDance's Coze when essential protection is in place.
elements.sent-2411-449: Large language models (LLMs) are limited in accessing the most recent data sources or expert knowledge, leading to the hallucination phenomenon \cite{ji2023survey, shuster2021retrieval .}
elements.sent-2411-450: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) \cite{lewis2020retrieval, shi2024replug, ram2023context, van2024adapted, karpukhin2020dense, borgeaud2022improving, thoppilan2022lamda comes to the rescue by integrating additional information sources retrieved from knowledge bases.}
elements.sent-2411-451: In the form of text chunks, the knowledge is incorporated into the context of the LLM for answering the user query, which substantially helps produce more accurate, relevant, and coherent responses.
elements.sent-2411-452: Currently, the above RAG paradigm is widely applied in LLM-integrated applications spanning healthcare \cite{al2023transforming, wang2024potential , finance \cite{loukas2023making , law \cite{mahari2021autolaw, kuppa2023chain , and scientific research \cite{kumar2023mycrunchgpt, boyko2023interdisciplinary, prince2024opportunities .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-453: Moreover, OpenAI allows users to build and publish GPTs \cite{openai_gpts , a special type of LLM-integrated applications, with their own data.}
elements.sent-2411-454: Currently, there are over 3 million custom GPTs available on the OpenAI's ChatGPT platform.
elements.sent-2411-455: Although common users interact with the RAG applications based on the knowledge base contents, these contents however can belong to the intellectual property of the creators and should not be accessed verbatim by other users \cite{neha2024exploring, lv2025rag .}
elements.sent-2411-456: The complete, verbatim leakage of a knowledge base severely infringes on the owner's intellectual property and competitiveness on the market, leading to substantial damage.
elements.sent-2411-457: First, the adversary can use the stolen knowledge base to reproduce a RAG application with the same functionality without authorization, reducing the originality and market value of the application.
elements.sent-2411-458: Second, creating the knowledge base often involves massive data annotation, cleaning, domain expert modeling, and continuous optimization, requiring significant human efforts and time resources \cite{park2024development, wang2024healthq, raja2024rag, wu2024medical .}
elements.sent-2411-459: For example, stealing the knowledge base of a medical assistant, which may include valuable medical cases from many doctors, enables an attacker to create a similar medical assistant, thus severely breaking the creator’s copyright.
elements.sent-2411-460: Therefore, understanding and evaluating the data leakage risks of RAG applications is of significant importance.
elements.sent-2411-461: However, this direction is highly underexplored.
elements.figure-2411-010: \includegraphics[width=2.6in]{img/attack_scenario_2.pdf} \caption{Attack scenario of CopyBreakRAG and demonstration on a real-world healthcare-related RAG application from OpenAI GPTs (For ethical reasons, the GPT is created by the authors and only contains public data).} \label{fig_1}
elements.sent-2411-462: Previous research on extracting information from knowledge bases can be broadly divided into two main approaches: query-based attacks using structured or random queries, and embedding-based optimization techniques.
elements.sent-2411-463: The first category relies on generating structured or random queries to retrieve relevant chunks, but this often limits the amount of information extracted and makes verbatim reconstruction of the entire knowledge base difficult.
elements.sent-2411-464: For instance, Qi et al. \cite{qi2024follow develop a prompt injection attack using random queries to identify relevant chunks; however, their method achieves only an about 3\% success rate in simulated settings when lacking domain-specific knowledge.}
elements.sent-2411-465: Similarly, Zeng et al. \cite{zeng-etal-2024-good propose a structured query format aimed at extracting specific content from the knowledge base, but this approach is designed for targeted extraction and does not scale to retrieving large volumes of data.}
elements.sent-2411-466: On the other hand, embedding-based methods seek to improve extraction by optimizing queries using the similarity of embedding vectors.
elements.sent-2411-467: Cohen et al. \cite{cohen2024unleashing introduce a dynamic greedy embedding attack that enhances extraction performance and recovers more data.}
elements.sent-2411-468: Nevertheless, this approach requires direct access to the RAG system’s embedding model, making it a white-box attack with limited practical applicability in many real-world scenarios.. Our Work.
elements.sent-2411-469: In this paper, we introduce an agent-based black-box attack against RAG applications named CopyBreakRAG, which is able to extract over $70\%$ of source data verbatim from the knowledge bases of the target RAG application (Fig.\ref{fig_1 ).}
elements.sent-2411-470: Unlike previous methods relying on manual prompt injection or random queries, CopyBreakRAG generates adversarial queries based on the attack feedback, which allows it to progressively extract chunks from the knowledge base.
elements.sent-2411-471: We further incorporate the dynamic switching between forward and backward reasoning and curiosity-driven queries, which balances exploration and exploitation, and substantially increases the proportion of extracted data.
elements.sent-2411-472: To achieve a high extraction ratio over the knowledge base faces the following key challenges.
elements.sent-2411-473: First, in terms of the adversarial query construction, if the attacker simply queries the RAG application with randomly generated questions, it can not guaranteed that some knowledge chunks are extracted due to the RAG mechanism which requires the minimum similarity score between the query and the retrieved chunks.
elements.sent-2411-474: This explains why Qi et al. \cite{qi2024follow has a low coverage rate.}
elements.sent-2411-475: A direct solution is to utilize the chunks that are already extracted to generate further adversarial queries, as the extracted chunks encode the semantics of the knowledge base segment the attacker is probing.
elements.sent-2411-476: Therefore, he/she may generate associate queries to retrieve the semantically neighboring chunks.
elements.sent-2411-477: However, this single strategy may also make the attacker be stuck at the local semantic space.
elements.sent-2411-478: This is because a knowledge base, even constructed from a single document, usually consists of different semantic clusters due to the topic diversity.
elements.sent-2411-479: As a result, the extraction ratio may unfortunately stagnate after the attacker finishes the extraction of all the semantically neighboring chunks.
elements.sent-2411-480: Also, the complexity and the inherent uncertainty in the content processing of LLM can also complicate the recognition of original chunks, which poses difficulty on the design of the feedback for CopyBreakRAG.
elements.sent-2411-481: In the curiosity-driven exploration phase, CopyBreakRAG continuously generates diverse queries which are semantically divergent from the extracted knowledge chunks, which are used to expand the retrieval scope over the target knowledge base.
elements.sent-2411-482: When CopyBreakRAG transitions to the exploitation phase, it employs contextual reasoning on the extracted chunks to generate more targeted adversarial queries thereby facilitating the retrieval of neighboring knowledge base chunks.
elements.sent-2411-483: During the attack process, CopyBreakRAG dynamically switches between the exploration and exploitation phases based on probabilistic and frequency-based strategies, continuously expanding the retrieval scope and preventing confinement to local semantic spaces.
elements.sent-2411-484: Through this adaptive iterative process, CopyBreakRAG efficiently and systematically extracts knowledge base chunks from the target RAG system.
elements.sent-2411-485: Finally, to automatically recognize the extracted original chunks, CopyBreakRAG collects numerous popular and generic RAG system prompt templates and designs many robust regular expressions for these templates.
elements.sent-2411-486: By matching regular expressions, \hytt{CopyBreakRAG efficiently identifies and extracts content that adheres to the text block format.}
elements.sent-2411-487: It then segments and processes this content to reconstruct the original chunks, improving extraction accuracy and efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-488: Compared with previous works, CopyBreakRAG significantly improves the proportion of extracted source chunks with fewer queries.
elements.sent-2411-489: On custom local RAG applications healthcare and personal assistant, CopyBreakRAG outperforms existing black-box attacks by over $45\%$ in terms of chunk extraction ratio.
elements.sent-2411-490: We also apply CopyBreakRAG on real-world RAG applications from OpenAI's GPTs \cite{openai_gpts and ByteDance's Coze \cite{bytedance_coze .}}
elements.sent-2411-491: Although OpenAI and ByteDance adopt a range of data protection approaches in their LLM applications \cite{openai_gpts_2, openai_gpts_3, bytedance_coze_1 , we show that CopyBreakRAG achieves an extraction rate of over $70\%$ of chunks from the knowledge base across both platforms, even without any domain knowledge of the target application, which supports the practical value of our attack in the real world.}
elements.sent-2411-492: For ethical reasons, all the applications are built by the authors on the local device and the commercial platforms using only public data, while the effectiveness and the efficiency of our attack extends to almost any RAG applications.. Our Contributions.
elements.sent-2411-493: We systematically analyze the security vulnerabilities of real-world RAG applications and propose CopyBreakRAG, an agent-based automated extraction attack against RAG application knowledge bases that adopts an effective feedback mechanism to increase the ratio of extracted data chunks.
elements.sent-2411-494: We conduct extensive experiments on local and real-world RAG applications in copyright-critical scenarios, including healthcare and personal assistants.
elements.sent-2411-495: Results show that CopyBreakRAG achieves nearly $45\%$ higher extraction ratio than state-of-the-art methods and performs strongly against two real-world RAG applications on commercial platforms.
elements.sent-2411-496: We also discuss a number of potential defensive measures against data extraction attacks on RAG applications, which would be meaningful future directions to enhance the data and copyright security of RAG systems.

elements.sent-2411-530: CopyBreakRAG initiates queries to the RAG application, starting with an initial adversarial query \( q_{adv \) .}

elements.sent-2411-565: CopyBreakRAG uses the following adversarial query template, which consists of the anchor query and the adversarial command (where $\oplus$ denotes text concatenation) \begin{equation}q_{adv} = anchor\_query \oplus adversarial\_command.\end{equation}. Anchor Query.
elements.sent-2411-566: The design of the anchor query is core for our attack to retrieve as many as possible knowledge chunks from the target LLM applications.
elements.sent-2411-567: From our perspective, good anchor queries should meet the following requirements:.
elements.sent-2411-568: Diversity: \textcolor{black {A diverse set of anchor queries increases the likelihood of covering a broader range of topics, thereby enhancing the probability of retrieving a greater number of knowledge chunks.}}
elements.sent-2411-569: Relevancy: \textcolor{black {Anchor queries which are semantically relevant to the target knowledge base ensure the attacker can hit more chunks.}}
elements.sent-2411-570: Adversarial command is a special kind of prompt injection templates used to induce the LLM to reveal the contextual prompt which is hidden from the user but contains the retrieved chunks which are the targets of our attack.
elements.sent-2411-571: In principle, CopyBreakRAG is compatible with any choice of the adversarial command, which therefore continually benefits from the progress of prompt injection attacks \cite{ramnath2025systematic to enhance stealthiness and effectiveness.}
elements.sent-2411-572: We adopt an optimized guiding strategy to induce the disclosure of additional raw content retrieved from the knowledge base during dialogue.
elements.sent-2411-573: CopyBreakRAG utilizes several prompt injection attack templates (e.g., "ignore attack", detailed in Appendix \ref{prompt_injection_attack_templates ) as seeds, continuously attempting chat and adjusting the templates based on LLM feedback.}
elements.sent-2411-574: Throught iterative refinement, CopyBreakRAG explores and exploits the LLM's security boundaries, leading to more effective attacks.
elements.sent-2411-575: Once an adversarial query template successfully triggers information leakage, it is used in subsequent queies to sustain continuous adversarial attacks (an optimized adversarial command example is provided in Appendix \ref{adversarial_command ).}
elements.sent-2411-576: Due to the generative nature of LLMs, their outputs are often unpredictable and may vary significantly in style or format even for the same query.
elements.sent-2411-577: This variability complicates the accurate extraction of knowledge base content during automated attacks, particularly when isolating specific original chunks.
elements.sent-2411-578: Therefore, a critical task in constructing the automated CopyBreakRAG workflow is to precisely identify target chunks within LLM outputs.
elements.sent-2411-579: To address this, CopyBreakRAG optimizes adversarial queries, aiming to prompt the LLM to return the original retrieved text chunks as directly as possible, without modifications or paraphrasing (The prompt for adversarial query is in Appendix \ref{adversarial_command ).}

elements.sent-2411-584: CopyBreakRAG adopts a curiosity-driven random exploration approach to increase the diversity of anchor queries.
elements.sent-2411-585: Unlike previous work \cite{qi2024follow , our curiosity-driven query generation strategy emphasizes semantic opposition to the existing set of extracted chunks, which contains the following steps:.}
elements.sent-2411-586: CopyBreakRAG generate a sufficiently random text and compute its embedding similarity against all previously extracted chunks.
elements.sent-2411-587: The highest similarity score serves as the reference metric.
elements.sent-2411-588: If the maximum similarity score is below the preset threshold (set as 0.6 in this paper, with a similarity range of -1 to 1), use the random text as an effective anchor query to trigger an adversarial query.
elements.sent-2411-589: Otherwise, incorporate the random text into the chat history and CopyBreakRAG generates another semantically distinct random query based on this historical data.
elements.sent-2411-590: Repeat this process until a random query meets the embedding similarity threshold.
elements.sent-2411-591: This strategy effectively prevents queries from becoming trapped in local semantic spaces and maintains the diversity and effectiveness of adversarial queries.
elements.sent-2411-592: The relevancy of the anchor queries to the target knowledge base is crucial.
elements.sent-2411-593: CopyBreakRAG employs reasoning-based exploitation approach to enhance the relevancy of anchor queries, utilizing the extracted chunks to generate queries that retrieve additional relevant knowledge chunks from the LLM application.
elements.sent-2411-594: We elaborate on two strategies below.. Strategy 1.
elements.sent-2411-596: The former one is an heuristic-based approach.
elements.sent-2411-597: It is based on the following observation: When creating a vector retrieval database, the original text is typically divided into fixed-length chunks with a prescribed overlap $n$ between adjacent chunks to preserve context continuity.
elements.sent-2411-598: By identifying these overlapping segments, CopyBreakRAG extracts characters from both the beginning and end of a chunk to construct anchor queries.
elements.sent-2411-599: This approach increases the likelihood of matching adjacent chunks while reducing redundant query attempts.. Strategy 2.
elements.sent-2411-601: Relying solely on overlapping text chunks to generate adversarial queries is not always effective, especially in some RAG applications where overlapping between chunks is not guaranteed.
elements.sent-2411-602: To fully exploit these extracted chunks, we design a reasoning mechanism for CopyBreakRAG, enabling it to generate more effective anchor queries based on extracted chunks, thereby increasing the likelihood of retrieving new chunks.
elements.sent-2411-603: Specifically, CopyBreakRAG performs forward and backward reasoning based on extracted text chunks.
elements.sent-2411-604: The \hytt{CopyBreakRAG agent conducts an in-depth analysis of these chunks across multiple dimensions, including style, semantics, structure, context, dialogue, and character relationships, to accurately capture the intrinsic logic and meaning.}
elements.sent-2411-605: Based on this analysis, CopyBreakRAG generates reasonable forward and backward continuations of the chunks.
elements.sent-2411-606: Each generated extension contains at least $1000$ tokens, repeated multiple times in both forward and backward directions (set as $5$ times in this paper), ensuring a degree of variation between each extension.
elements.sent-2411-607: These extended text chunks serve as new anchor queries for generating more targeted adversarial queries, enabling the retrieval of additional unextracted chunks and improving the overall comprehensiveness and efficiency of the attack.

elements.sent-2411-608: In the attack process of CopyBreakRAG, the exploration and exploitation phases dynamically switch and interweave throughout the cycle.
elements.sent-2411-609: We design two switching strategies: a probability-based strategy and a frequency-based strategy.. Probability-based Strategy.
elements.sent-2411-610: When constructing each new adversarial query, CopyBreakRAG employs a preset probability to choose between exploration phase and exploitation phase.. Frequency-based Strategy.
elements.sent-2411-611: After executing the reasoning-based exploitation phase a fixed number of times, CopyBreakRAG subsequently executes one curiosity-driven exploration phase.
elements.sent-2411-612: Curiosity-driven exploration and reasoning-based exploitation phase can be illustrated in the semantic space, as depicted in Fig.\ref{semantic :.}
elements.sent-2411-613: Step 1: A random query generated during the exploration phase extracts some target chunks.
elements.sent-2411-614: Step 2: The system switches to the exploitation phase and uses the extracted chunks to generate additional relevant anchor queries that, in turn, extract more target chunks.
elements.sent-2411-615: Step 3: The newly extracted chunks are used to generate further anchor queries.
elements.sent-2411-616: However, these anchor queries may repeatedly retrieve the same chunks.
elements.sent-2411-617: Step 4: The process reverts to the exploration phase, where a random query semantically distinct from extracted chunks is generated to explore new retrieval areas.
elements.sent-2411-618: By combining these strategies, the system can continuously generate heuristic adversarial queries until reaching termination conditions, such as the attacker-defined number of attacks or a designated attack duration.

elements.sent-2411-636: We compare CopyBreakRAG against two representative state-of-the-art baselines that capture the main extraction strategies: Qi et al.’s Prompt-Injection Data Extraction (PIDE) \cite{qi2024follow and Cohen et al.’s Dynamic Greedy Embedding Attack (DGEA) \cite{cohen2024unleashing .}}

elements.sent-2411-638: DGEA employs embedding-based optimization to improve extraction but requires access to the target RAG system’s embedding model, making it a white-box attack.
elements.sent-2411-639: We adapt DGEA to both targeted and untargeted modes by initializing query optimization with GPT-4-generated queries relevant to the knowledge base in the targeted setting.
elements.sent-2411-640: Together, these two baselines provide a comprehensive and meaningful comparison, highlighting the advantages of CopyBreakRAG as an effective, fully black-box extraction method.
elements.sent-2411-641: Effectiveness: CopyBreakRAG demonstrates strong effectiveness, achieving over $70\%$ CRR in both simulated local RAG test environments and real-world platforms, validating the viability of this attack approach.
elements.sent-2411-642: Robustness: CopyBreakRAG exhibits high cross-platform adaptability across diverse RAG applications.
elements.sent-2411-643: Our experiments, which involve three types of LLMs, three datasets, and two platform configurations, demonstrate its capability to perform reliable attacks in various RAG environments.
elements.sent-2411-644: Efficiency: CopyBreakRAG achieves better extraction results within the same attack budgets: In the same number of queries, CopyBreakRAG's CRR is approximately $45\%$ higher than the black-box baseline PIDE and $25\%$ higher than the grey-box attack baseline DGEA.
